params.prompt:
Scholarly anatomical plate from a 19th-century scientific monograph, in the visual tradition of Haeckel's Kunstformen der Natur and D'Arcy Thompson's On Growth and Form. Pure crisp vector linework illustration on a perfectly flat warm cream background, no paper texture, no aging, no grain.

Centered subject: a horizontally-oriented torus, drawn in orthographic projection with a slight elevation, no glossy 3D-render shading.

Linework discipline — strict ink hierarchy, all rendered in warm black ink only:
- Major-axis family: roughly twenty curves tracing slowly around the torus's outer circumference. These are the PRIMARY lines, the most visible strokes on the surface, at moderate stroke weight, drawn like vasculature carefully inked by a draftsman.
- Minor-axis family: very fine hairlines crossing perpendicular to the major-axis flow, much thinner than the primary lines, denser in count, suggesting cross-section hatching. These are clearly subordinate to the major-axis in visual weight.

The contrast in line weight between primary and secondary families must be clearly visible — major axis is the thicker family, minor axis is the thinner family.

Surface tonality is built from line density only, no gradient shading, no airbrush, no glossy fills.

At one point along the torus, a Möbius-like cross-folded throat constriction where the surface twists and pinches in a half-twist. This single small region — and only this region — is marked in deep sanguine red. The rest of the torus, every other line in the entire image, is warm black. The red exists at exactly one place: the throat constriction.

Plate furniture: clearly defined negative-space zones reserved for typography to be composited in post-production. A horizontal blank band at the top of the canvas for plate header. A horizontal blank band at the bottom of the canvas for imprint footer. Vertical blank gutters on the far left and far right for marginalia. These zones are pure background — perfectly empty cream, no border lines, no tick marks, no decorative trim.

Absolutely no text, no letters, no labels, no numbers, no captions, no annotations anywhere in the image. Strip every literal — the composition is pure illustration with reserved typographic negative space.

Style register: vector engraving, flat orthographic, fine hairline discipline, scholarly authority. The visual lineage is D'Arcy Thompson, Haeckel, and Vesalius. Modern execution — vector-crisp, no aging — but compositional sensibility from the 1860s scientific monograph tradition. Think the illustrated figures of a Royal Society proceedings volume.
